original_alert.title: TEST-Manual-1759005842061
original_alert.state_reason: Manual test trigger via API endpoint

alert_examples.ai_formatted_alert:
Alert: TEST-Manual-1759005842061 - Manually triggered test incident
Severity: LOW
Source: AWS CloudWatch
Location: us-east-1
Reason: Manual test trigger via API endpoint
Status: open
alert_examples.expert_response:
This is a manual test alert with LOW severity from AWS CloudWatch in us-east-1.

**Immediate Actions:**
1. Verify this is indeed a test alert by checking the title "TEST-Manual"
2. Confirm with the team who triggered this manual test
3. Document the test results if this was planned

**Investigation:**
- Check if this is part of scheduled testing procedures
- Verify AWS CloudWatch alarm configuration is working correctly
- Review if the alert routing and notification systems are functioning

**Resolution:**
- Since this is a test alert with LOW severity, acknowledge and close
- Update test documentation with results
- Consider scheduling regular test alerts during maintenance windows

**Prevention:**
- Implement clear naming conventions for test alerts
- Set up separate test alert channels to avoid confusion
- Document all manual test procedures

original_alert.title: High CPU Usage - prod-web-01
original_alert.description: CPU utilization has exceeded 90% for 5 consecutive minutes

alert_examples.ai_formatted_alert:
Alert: High CPU Usage - prod-web-01 - CPU utilization has exceeded 90% for 5 consecutive minutes
Severity: HIGH
Source: AWS CloudWatch
Location: us-east-1
Reason: CPUUtilization metric exceeded threshold of 90%
Status: open
alert_examples.expert_response:
Critical CPU alert on prod-web-01 requiring immediate investigation:

**Immediate Actions:**
1. SSH into prod-web-01 and check current CPU usage: `top -n 1`
2. Identify top CPU-consuming processes: `ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -20`
3. Check system load: `uptime` and `iostat 1 5`
4. Review recent deployments or changes in the last 2 hours

**Investigation:**
- Monitor application logs: `tail -f /var/log/application/*.log`
- Check for runaway processes or memory leaks
- Review CloudWatch metrics for patterns (disk I/O, memory, network)
- Verify auto-scaling policies are functioning

**Resolution:**
- Kill any identified runaway processes if safe to do so
- Restart problematic services: `sudo systemctl restart service-name`
- Scale horizontally if traffic-related: Add more instances
- Consider vertical scaling if resource-constrained

**Prevention:**
- Set up proactive monitoring at 75% threshold
- Implement auto-scaling based on CPU metrics
- Regular performance profiling of applications
- Monitor for gradual CPU increases over time

original_alert.title: Database Connection Pool Exhausted
original_alert.description: RDS connection pool has reached maximum capacity (100/100)
original_alert.state_reason: DatabaseConnections metric exceeded maximum of 100 connections

alert_examples.ai_formatted_alert:
Alert: Database Connection Pool Exhausted - RDS connection pool has reached maximum capacity (100/100)
Severity: CRITICAL
Source: AWS RDS
Location: us-east-1
Reason: DatabaseConnections metric exceeded maximum of 100 connections
Status: open
alert_examples.expert_response:
Critical database connectivity crisis requiring urgent attention:

**Immediate Actions:**
1. Check RDS CloudWatch metrics for connection patterns
2. Identify applications with most connections: `SHOW PROCESSLIST;` on RDS
3. Kill long-running idle connections if safe: `KILL CONNECTION <id>;`
4. Scale connection limits temporarily if possible

**Investigation:**
- Review application connection pooling configuration
- Check for connection leaks in application code
- Monitor slow query log for blocking queries
- Verify connection timeout settings across all services

**Resolution:**
- Restart applications with suspected connection leaks
- Implement connection pooling best practices (HikariCP, PgBouncer)
- Scale RDS instance class for more connections
- Set up connection limit monitoring per application

**Prevention:**
- Implement proper connection pool sizing (10-20 per app instance)
- Add connection pool monitoring to deployment pipeline
- Set up automated alerts at 80% connection usage
- Regular code review for connection handling practices

original_alert.title: SSL Certificate Expiring Soon
original_alert.description: SSL certificate for api.company.com expires in 7 days

alert_examples.ai_formatted_alert:
Alert: SSL Certificate Expiring Soon - SSL certificate for api.company.com expires in 7 days
Severity: MEDIUM
Source: Certificate Monitor
Location: global
Reason: Certificate expires on 2025-10-04T22:00:00.000Z
Status: open
alert_examples.expert_response:
SSL certificate renewal required - action needed within a week:

**Immediate Actions:**
1. Verify certificate details: `openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout | grep 'Not After'`
2. Check auto-renewal status: `certbot certificates`
3. Test certificate renewal: `certbot renew --dry-run`
4. Schedule manual renewal if auto-renewal is broken

**Investigation:**
- Check certbot logs: `/var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log`
- Verify domain DNS settings and ownership validation
- Review load balancer certificate configuration
- Confirm certificate is deployed across all environments

**Resolution:**
- Manual renewal: `certbot renew --force-renewal -d api.company.com`
- Update load balancer with new certificate
- Update CDN/proxy configurations
- Test all API endpoints after certificate update

**Prevention:**
- Set up 30-day and 14-day expiration warnings
- Implement automated certificate renewal and deployment
- Monitor certificate status in CI/CD pipeline
- Maintain certificate inventory with expiration dates

original_alert.title: High Network Latency Detected
original_alert.description: Network latency between services exceeds 200ms threshold

alert_examples.ai_formatted_alert:
Alert: High Network Latency Detected - Network latency between services exceeds 200ms threshold
Severity: HIGH
Source: Network Monitor
Location: us-east-1
Reason: Inter-service latency averaged 250ms over 5 minutes
Status: open
alert_examples.expert_response:
Network performance degradation affecting service communication:

**Immediate Actions:**
1. Check network connectivity: `ping` and `traceroute` between services
2. Monitor network interface statistics: `netstat -i` and `ss -tuln`
3. Review AWS VPC flow logs for unusual patterns
4. Check for network congestion or packet loss

**Investigation:**
- Review CloudWatch network metrics (NetworkIn/Out, NetworkPacketsIn/Out)
- Check for routing table changes or security group modifications
- Monitor bandwidth utilization between availability zones
- Verify no ongoing maintenance in AWS infrastructure

**Resolution:**
- Restart network services if interface issues detected
- Scale network capacity or change instance types if bandwidth-limited
- Implement connection pooling to reduce network overhead
- Consider deploying services closer together (same AZ)

**Prevention:**
- Set up network latency monitoring between critical services
- Implement circuit breakers for network-dependent operations
- Regular network performance baseline measurements
- Design for network failures with retry logic and timeouts

original_alert.title: Application Error Rate Spike
original_alert.description: Error rate for payment-service increased to 15% over last 10 minutes

alert_examples.ai_formatted_alert:
Alert: Application Error Rate Spike - Error rate for payment-service increased to 15% over last 10 minutes
Severity: HIGH
Source: Application Monitor
Location: us-east-1
Reason: HTTP 5xx error rate exceeded 10% threshold
Status: open
alert_examples.expert_response:
Critical application performance issue requiring immediate attention:

**Immediate Actions:**
1. Check payment-service logs: `kubectl logs deployment/payment-service --tail=100`
2. Review recent deployments: `kubectl rollout history deployment/payment-service`
3. Check service health endpoints: `curl http://payment-service/health`
4. Monitor database connectivity from payment-service

**Investigation:**
- Review application metrics dashboard for patterns
- Check external API dependencies (payment gateways, banks)
- Monitor resource usage (CPU, memory, disk) of payment-service pods
- Analyze error patterns: timeouts, database errors, or validation failures

**Resolution:**
- Rollback recent deployment if correlation found: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service`
- Scale service horizontally: `kubectl scale deployment payment-service --replicas=5`
- Restart unhealthy pods: `kubectl delete pods -l app=payment-service`
- Implement circuit breaker if external dependency issue

**Prevention:**
- Set up error rate alerts at 5% and 10% thresholds
- Implement comprehensive health checks for all dependencies
- Add canary deployments with automatic rollback
- Monitor and alert on payment gateway response times
